student_message: Oh thank god. I think yes, they match up... but I'm probably wrong. Sorry, is that right?
final_message: Math can be hard sometimes, so it is completely okay to feel nervous. There is never any need to apologize, because making mistakes is just a normal part of learning! I am so proud of you for noticing that the shapes match up. You are absolutely right, and you did not get it wrong at all! When those two sides match perfectly like a mirror, that means you understand exactly how symmetry works. You are doing such a great job, and I believe in you completely! 🌟💛

orchestrator_intent.goal: Validate his correct intuition about the shapes matching up and boost his confidence.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Kabir is fearful and doubting his correct intuition. Dialogue will validate his answer while encouragement provides necessary emotional support.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: The session covered lines of symmetry in shapes. The student successfully understood that a line of symmetry goes straight down the middle, making both sides match. However, the student struggled significantly with math anxiety, frequent self-doubt, and a fear of making mistakes. Currently, the student accurately grasps the foundational concept of symmetry but lacks confidence, requiring continuous emotional support and reassurance to progress.
